method3.generated_article:
==Geography and ecology==
Jarvis Island is an uninhabited coral island in the central equatorial Pacific Ocean, located about 40 km (25 mi) south of the equator at 0°22′S 160°01′W.[2] The island is part of the central Line Islands, also known as the Teraina Islands, and is the crest of an ancient coral reef cap over a massive extinct volcano.[2] Its emergent land area is 1,273 acres, while submerged lands and waters within the three-mile refuge boundary encompass 36,214 acres.[3] The island’s interior is a dried former lagoon containing deep guano deposits.[2]  

Jarvis has a tropical desert climate, with high daytime temperatures, steady easterly trade winds, intense equatorial sun, and scant rainfall of less than 1 m (40 in) per year.[2] The terrain is mostly sandy and low-lying, reaching only about 7 m (23 ft) at its highest point.[2] The island has no known natural freshwater lens and supports no plants larger than shrubs.[2] Its sparse vegetation consists mainly of bunch grasses, vines, and low-growing shrubs that provide nesting, roosting, and sheltering habitat for seabirds, shorebirds, and land hermit crabs.[2]  

The marine environment around Jarvis is highly productive because of wind-driven equatorial upwelling and topographic upwelling near the island.[2] Its shallow benthic habitats include a fringing reef, shallow back reefs, a steep forereef, and a moderate-sized reef terrace on the eastern windward side of the island.[2] The surrounding reef has been described by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service as a healthy coral community, reflecting Jarvis’s remoteness and limited anthropogenic disturbance.[3]  

Jarvis supports more nesting seabirds than any other island in the U.S. tropical Pacific Islands.[2] The island has been identified as having 14 species of breeding seabirds and as an important protected seabird island in the northern Line Islands.[3] Jarvis alone has been estimated to host about 1 million sooty terns.[1] The island’s terrestrial and marine ecosystems are managed with emphasis on preserving the complete ecosystem and protecting large seabird nesting colonies.[3]  

Introduced predators historically affected Jarvis’s bird populations, including feral cats, whose impacts and eradication were the subject of a study by Mark J. Rauzon.[4] After eradication efforts on Baker, Howland, and Jarvis, seabird recovery varied by island, with shearwaters noted as returning to Jarvis.[1] The refuge has also been described as a living laboratory for studying climate change, El Niño–Southern Oscillation, and other ecological processes in relatively intact island, coral reef, and deep marine habitats.[3]

==History==
The first known European sighting of Jarvis Island occurred on August 21, 1821, when Captain Brown of the British ship *Eliza Francis* reached the island; it was named for the ship’s owners, Edward, Thomas, and William Jarvis.[2][4] The island was also formerly known as Bunker Island or Bunker’s Shoal.[4]

American whalers and other seafarers later noted guano on Jarvis, leading the United States Exploring Expedition to survey the island in 1840.[2] In 1857, Benson and Judd, aboard the Hawaiian schooner *Liholiho*, took possession of Jarvis for the American Guano Company under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2][4] Later that year, Commander Charles Henry Davis of the USS *St. Mary* surveyed the island and formally claimed it for the United States.[4] Guano mining continued into the late 19th century, and by the time the United States abandoned Jarvis in 1879, about 8,000 tonnes of mined guano had reportedly been collected.[1]

The United Kingdom annexed Jarvis in 1889, but did not develop new mining operations there.[4][5] In 1906, the island was leased to the Pacific Phosphate Company of London, although mining again was not further developed.[4] The United States reoccupied and reclaimed Jarvis in 1935, restoring it to U.S. control.[1][4][5] On May 13, 1936, President Franklin D. Roosevelt signed Executive Order 7368, placing control and jurisdiction of the island under the Secretary of the Interior.[3]

During World War II, Japanese forces attacked Jarvis in 1942, leading to the evacuation of the island’s colonists.[1] The island was later administered by the Department of the Interior, and on June 27, 1974, the Secretary of the Interior designated Jarvis Island and its territorial sea out to 3 nautical miles as a unit of the National Wildlife Refuge System.[3] In 2009, Jarvis Island was also included in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[5]

==Transportation==
Jarvis Island is accessible only by ship; open-ocean vessels capable of extended travel are described as the sole means of visiting the island.[3] The island lies about 1,263 nautical miles from management staff in Honolulu, and the voyage to Jarvis takes about eight days on average.[3] Recent transportation has been provided by NOAA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and private charter vessels.[3] Scientific and management visits are commonly incorporated into wider central Pacific expeditions that also include other remote island refuges such as Baker, Howland, and Johnston.[3] Logistical challenges include the island’s remoteness, limited affordable and reliable transportation, lack of field infrastructure, extreme environmental conditions, and safety concerns associated with landing and operations.[3] The island is low-lying and surrounded by a narrow shallow fringing reef, with a sandy leeward western beach and a more wave-exposed eastern shore covered with coral rubble and sandstone slabs.[1]

==Military==
Jarvis's principal military significance came from its inclusion in the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, which placed U.S. citizens on Jarvis, Howland and Baker so that weather stations and landing fields could be developed for military and commercial air routes between Australia and California.[1] The project also had a strategic purpose, strengthening U.S. claims to remote central Pacific islands as a check on Japanese territorial expansion eastward.[1] From 1935 to early 1942, Jarvis was staffed as part of this effort by rotating parties of young men recruited largely from Hawaii; a later U.S. Army account noted that Jarvis, Howland and Baker together served as temporary homes for more than 130 such colonists.[2] Physical traces of military-era use include short channels blasted through the shallow fringing reef to improve small-boat access, remnants of which have been documented in the western and eastern reef-terrace areas.[4] Refuge planning documents also identify possible residual hazards from human occupation during the guano-mining and military periods as a management concern for the otherwise uninhabited island.[3]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
The refuge’s stated purpose at transfer to the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service was “the preservation of the complete ecosystem, terrestrial as well as marine,” with special emphasis on large seabird nesting colonies.[5] Its original refuge boundary included the island and its territorial sea to the three-mile limit, totaling 37,487 acres, of which 1,273 acres were emergent land and 36,214 acres were submerged lands and waters.[5] The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service later described the refuge as encompassing 429,853 acres, including 1,273 terrestrial acres and 428,580 submerged acres, while the World Database on Protected Areas reports an area of 1,744.87 km².[2][1]  

Management is guided by the National Wildlife Refuge System mission to conserve, manage, and, where appropriate, restore fish, wildlife, plants, and their habitats for the benefit of present and future generations.[2][5] Refuge planning documents identify goals including conserving diverse wildlife and plant communities, protecting habitats for migratory birds and other interjurisdictional resources, and maintaining the ecological character of the site.[5] The refuge is administered within the Pacific Islands Heritage Marine National Monument framework, which includes Jarvis Island National Wildlife Refuge within its boundaries.[2]  

Access is tightly managed because refuge uses must be compatible with conservation purposes; in one draft compatibility determination, the Service evaluated limited amateur radio access and concluded that it would not detract from protection of the refuge’s natural character, wildlife, plants, coral reef communities, tidal lands, submerged lands, and waters.[2] U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service biological teams continue to conduct intermittent field visits, including a 2024 visit described as the agency’s first trip to Jarvis in more than six years.[3]
